Launch Cortex Desktop and open a workspace. 2. Open **Settings**. 3. Locate the **Search settings** input field at the top of the settings list. 4. Hover the mouse over the input/placeholder area and wait briefly. ### Expected Behavior - Hovering the search input should show tooltip/help text (or `title`/accessible description), for example: - Repeat/clarify placeholder usage, - Explain available filter tokens like `@modified`, `@ext`, `@lang`, `@tag`. ### Actual Behavior - No tooltip appears when hovering the settings search input. - Users get no hover help for placeholder/filter usage. ### Additional Context _No response_

items.title: [BUG] [alpha] Quick Access Extensions hovering a row changes the Enter target without clicking
items.body:
### Project

ide

### Description

In **Quick Access -> Extensions**, merely hovering an extension row changes which item `Enter` will activate.

Visible result:

- open **Quick Access -> Extensions**,
- move selection with the keyboard,
- hover a different extension row with the mouse without clicking,
- press `Enter`,
- the hovered row is activated instead of the previously keyboard-selected row.

So the mounted field lets passive mouse hover silently retarget the primary keyboard action.

### Root Cause

The Quick Access keyboard handler always activates the current `selectedIndex` on `Enter`:

- `/src/context/QuickAccessContext.tsx:1216-1233`
  - `Enter` resolves `currentItems[selectedIndex()]`
  - then calls `handleAccept(selected)`

The row renderer mutates that same `selectedIndex` on hover, even without a click:

- `/src/context/QuickAccessContext.tsx:1534-1538`
  - `onMouseEnter` calls `setSelectedIndex(index())`
  - it also sets `hoveredIndex(index())`

So moving the pointer across a row is enough to replace the keyboard-selected target.

In **Quick Access -> Extensions**, that matters because row activation is not just navigation; it performs an immediate extension-state action:

- `/src/context/QuickAccessContext.tsx:1108-1116`
  - extension rows of type `show` immediately call `enableExtension(...)` or `disableExtension(...)`

That makes a non-click hover capable of changing which extension the next `Enter` will toggle.


### Error Message

```shell

```

### Debug Logs

```shell

```

### System Information

```shell
- Cortex IDE `alpha`
- Platform: any
```

### Screenshots

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/d81c78e5-d2d7-4230-99aa-ea4ec6ca836d

### Steps to Reproduce

1. Open **Quick Access**.
2. Switch to **Extensions** mode.
3. Use arrow keys to select one extension row.
4. Move the mouse over a different extension row without clicking.
5. Press `Enter`.
6. Observe which extension row is activated.


### Expected Behavior

Keyboard selection should remain stable until the user changes it intentionally.

Hovering a different row without clicking should not retarget the next `Enter` action.


### Actual Behavior


Hovering a different extension row changes `selectedIndex` immediately.

The next `Enter` acts on the hovered row even though the user never clicked it.


### Additional Context

This is not the same as the current live Quick Access Extensions issues:

- `#33291` **Quick Access Extensions search ignores visible Enabled or Disabled status text**
- `#33403` **Quick Access Extensions search ignores visible action labels like Install Extension and Refresh Extensions**
- `#33414` **Quick Access Extensions pin button has no effect on result ordering or recall**
- `#33417` **Quick Access Extensions failed enable or disable closes the picker with no visible error**
- `#33422` **Quick Access Extensions selecting an extension row toggles enable or disable instead of showing extension details**
- `#33438` **Quick Access Extensions has no uninstall action for installed extensions**
- `#33444` **Quick Access Extensions does not remember recent selections**
- `#33451` **Quick Access Extensions Install Extension action opens the installed extensions tab instead of marketplace**
- `#33460` **Quick Access Extensions can dead-end in No results found for not-installed extension names**
- `#33472` **Quick Access Extensions can reopen with stale Enabled or Disabled state while a toggle is still in flight**
- `#33477` **Quick Access Extensions Refresh Extensions closes the picker instead of refreshing results in place**
- `#33482` **Quick Access Extensions can show No results found before deferred extension loading finishes**
- `#33487` **Quick Access Extensions omits the Show Installed Extensions action defined by the extension provider**
- `#33492` **Quick Access Extensions pin button is mouse-only and unreachable from keyboard**
- `#33501` **Quick Access Extensions can reopen with a stale pin button visible on a non-hovered row**

Those reports cover search mismatches, wrong destinations, stale state, pin behavior, missing actions, and row activation semantics.

This report is specifically about passive hover changing the keyboard `Enter` target without a click.

### Duplicate Check

Re-checked local (`bug-3`, `bugs-codex`) and targeted live repo searches with:

- `Quick Access Extensions hover Enter`
- `Quick Access Extensions hovering row changes selection`
- `Quick Access Extensions hover changes Enter target`
- `Quick Access Extensions mouse hover retargets`
- local grep for `case "Enter"`, `setSelectedIndex(index())`, and the extension-row `show` action in `/src/context/QuickAccessContext.tsx`

Findings:

- Local: no matching report found for this specific **Quick Access Extensions hovering a row changes the Enter target without clicking** path.
- Live repo: no matching issue found in `PlatformNetwork/bounty-challenge` for this exact `Quick Access -> Extensions` hover-retargeting bug at check time (`2026-03-19 UTC`).
- Closest live issues are different:
  - `#33422` `Quick Access Extensions selecting an extension row toggles enable or disable instead of showing extension details`
  - `#33501` `Quick Access Extensions can reopen with a stale pin button visible on a non-hovered row`
- Why they are different:
  - `#33422` is about what row activation does once a row is selected,
  - `#33501` is about stale hover state persisting across sessions,
  - this report is about hover alone changing which row `Enter` will activate.

items.title: [BUG] [alpha] Prompt Store New Prompt command can overwrite the existing store without loading it first

labels.name: duplicate
labels.description: This issue or pull request already exists

items.body:
### Project

ide

### Description

The mounted **Prompt Store: New Prompt** command can open the editor before existing prompts and categories have been loaded.

Visible result:

- start the IDE with an existing Prompt Store library already saved,
- trigger **Prompt Store: New Prompt** before ever opening the Prompt Store panel,
- create and save a new prompt,
- the save runs against the still-unloaded in-memory state.

That means the newly saved state can overwrite the previously saved Prompt Store contents instead of appending to them.

### Root Cause

Prompt Store state starts empty and is intentionally lazy-loaded only when the panel is opened:

- `/src/context/PromptStoreContext.tsx:247-255`
  - initial `prompts: []`
  - initial default categories only
  - comment explicitly says prompts are lazy-loaded when the panel opens

The direct command registration wires **Prompt Store: New Prompt** to `createNewPrompt` rather than `openPanel`:

- `/src/context/PromptStoreContext.tsx:270-275`
  - command `prompt-store-new`
  - action: `createNewPrompt`

But `createNewPrompt()` does not load existing data first:

- `/src/context/PromptStoreContext.tsx:591-595`
  - clears editor state
  - sets `isCreatingNew = true`
  - sets `showPanel = true`
  - does not call `loadPrompts()`

The only lazy-load path is `openPanel()`:

- `/src/context/PromptStoreContext.tsx:572-577`
  - loads prompts only if `state.prompts.length === 0`

Saving a new prompt mutates the current in-memory store and then persists the entire current prompt/category state:

- `/src/context/PromptStoreContext.tsx:370-385`
  - `createPrompt()` appends to `state.prompts`
  - then calls `saveToStorage()`
- `/src/context/PromptStoreContext.tsx:339-346`
  - `saveToStorage()` persists `state.prompts` and `state.categories`

So if **Prompt Store: New Prompt** is used before the first load, save can persist an incomplete in-memory store containing only the newly created prompt plus default categories.


### Error Message

```shell

```

### Debug Logs

```shell

```

### System Information

```shell
- Cortex IDE `alpha`
- Platform: any
```

### Screenshots

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/f9b443b6-b06b-447b-8b2c-5e4f32fd6226

### Steps to Reproduce

1. Ensure Prompt Store already contains saved prompts and, ideally, custom categories.
2. Restart the IDE so Prompt Store state starts unloaded.
3. Trigger **Prompt Store: New Prompt** directly.
4. Create a new prompt and save it.
5. Open Prompt Store normally.
6. Inspect the prompt list and category list.


### Expected Behavior

Creating a new prompt through the direct command should first load the existing Prompt Store state, then append the new prompt to it.

Previously saved prompts and categories should remain intact.


### Actual Behavior

The direct **New Prompt** path can save before the existing store has ever been loaded.

That save can overwrite the persisted Prompt Store with incomplete in-memory state.


### Additional Context

This is not the same as the current live Prompt Store issues:

- `#20811` **AI: Prompt Store New Prompt command does nothing**
- `#24162` **Prompt Store allows creating multiple prompts with the same title without warning**
- `#24558` **Re-importing the same external prompt file creates duplicates every time**
- `#27033` **New prompt form defaults to category "general" which may not exist**
- `#27132` **Create Prompt modal has no focus trap**

Those reports cover command wiring, duplicate titles, import duplication, invalid default category, and modal accessibility.

This report is specifically about the direct **New Prompt** command bypassing the only load path and risking overwrite of the existing stored library.

### Duplicate Check

Re-checked local (`bug-3`, `bugs-codex`) and targeted live repo searches with:

- `Prompt Store new prompt load`
- `Prompt Store new prompt overwrites`
- `Prompt Store lazy load create prompt`
- `Prompt Store existing prompts lost`
- local grep for `prompt-store-new`, `createNewPrompt`, `openPanel`, `loadPrompts`, and `saveToStorage` in `/src/context/PromptStoreContext.tsx`

Findings:

- Local: no matching report found for this specific **Prompt Store New Prompt command can overwrite the existing store without loading it first** path.
- Live repo: no matching issue found in `PlatformNetwork/bounty-challenge` for this exact Prompt Store lazy-load overwrite path at check time (`2026-03-19 UTC`).
- Closest live issues are different:
  - `#20811` `AI: Prompt Store New Prompt command does nothing`
  - `#24558` `Re-importing the same external prompt file creates duplicates every time`
  - `#27033` `New prompt form defaults to category "general" which may not exist`
- Why they are different:
  - `#20811` is about a non-working command path,
  - `#24558` is about import duplication,
  - `#27033` is about the default category value,
  - this report is about bypassing the initial load and then persisting incomplete state over the existing library.

items.body:
**Expensify/Expensify Issue URL:** https://github.com/Expensify/Expensify/issues/603735 **Slack conversation:** https://expensify.slack.com/archives/C03U7DCU4/p1771944902661119?thread_ts=1771944902.661119&cid=C03U7DCU4 --- ## Action Performed 1. Open a report that has multiple expenses, where at least one expense has an RBR indicator (violation, hold, field error, receipt error, etc.) 2. Look at the expense carousel in the report preview ## Expected Result The expense that requires action (has RBR) should appear **first** in the carousel so the user can immediately identify and act on it. ## Actual Result Expenses appear in backend insertion order. RBR-flagged expenses can be buried at position 5+ in the carousel, making them easy to miss entirely — especially since only the first 11 are shown. ## Root Cause There is no RBR-aware sorting anywhere in the transaction display path: 1. **Carousel** — [`MoneyRequestReportPreviewContent.tsx:~506`](https://github.com/Expensify/App/blob/main/src/components/ReportActionItem/MoneyRequestReportPreview/MoneyRequestReportPreviewContent.tsx#L506) does `transactions.slice(0, 11)` with no sort beforehand. 2. **Report transaction list** — [`MoneyRequestReportTransactionList.tsx:~325`](https://github.com/Expensify/App/blob/main/src/components/MoneyRequestReportView/MoneyRequestReportTransactionList.tsx#L325) sorts by date/column only; `visualOrderTransactionIDs` (which drives RHP carousel navigation) inherits this non-RBR-aware order. The data flows from Onyx derived store → `Object.values()` (insertion order) → carousel/list — no step prioritizes actionable items. ## Proposed Fix Sort RBR-flagged transactions to the front in both locations: - **Carousel:** Sort `transactions` by RBR status before the `.slice(0, 11)` in `MoneyRequestReportPreviewContent`. - **Transaction list:** Use RBR status as a primary sort key in `MoneyRequestReportTransactionList`, with the existing date/column sort as secondary. ### Existing infrastructure to reuse - [`shouldShowRBR`](https://github.com/Expensify/App/blob/main/src/libs/TransactionPreviewUtils.ts#L411-L425) from `TransactionPreviewUtils.createTransactionPreviewConditionals()` - `filteredViolations` already computed per-transaction in `MoneyRequestReportTransactionList` (~lines 216-237) - Report-level RBR prioritization pattern in [`SidebarUtils.ts:~533`](https://github.com/Expensify/App/blob/main/src/libs/SidebarUtils.ts#L533-L535) ## Open Questions - Should RBR-first sorting override a user-selected column sort (date, merchant, etc.) in the report view, or only apply as the default? - Should sorting be centralized in `useReportWithTransactionsAndViolations` so all consumers benefit? ## Platforms - [x] Android: App - [x] Android: mWeb Chrome - [x] iOS: App - [x] iOS: mWeb Safari - [x] iOS: mWeb Chrome - [x] Windows: Chrome - [x] MacOS: Chrome / Safari

items.title: [Feature] Remove unused SM2 algorithm and related configuration
items.body:
## Background

The java-tron codebase contains a complete SM2 implementation in the `crypto` module. The `SignUtils.java` provides a configuration flag `isECKeyCryptoEngine` to switch between ECKey (SECP256K1) and SM2. Additionally, SM3 hash implementation exists alongside SM2.

The `crypto.engine` configuration option allows switching between cryptographic engines, but in practice only ECKey is ever used in production.

## Problem Statement

1. **SM2/SM3 is never used in production**: All production deployments use `ECKey (SECP256K1)`. The `isECKeyCryptoEngine` flag is always `true`.

2. **Dead code maintenance burden**: Maintaining ~1,100+ lines of unused cryptographic code (SM2 + SM3) increases complexity, testing surface, and audit scope.

3. **Incompatible with TRON network**: TRON uses `SECP256K1` for all transactions. SM2 would not work with existing accounts.

4. **Bug Bounty false positives**: The unused SM2 code frequently triggers false positive reports in bug bounty programs. Security researchers often flag SM2-related code as potential vulnerabilities, requiring time-consuming investigation and response.

5. **Unnecessary configuration complexity**: The `crypto.engine` config and `isECKeyCryptoEngine` checks exist throughout the codebase but only serve unused code paths.

## Rationale

### Why should this feature exist?

Removing unused code improves code quality and reduces maintenance overhead. Eliminating the `crypto.engine` configuration simplifies the codebase by removing conditional logic that never changes in production.

### What are the use cases?

1. Developers will have cleaner codebase to work with
2. Security audits will focus on actually-used cryptography
3. Fewer false positive reports in bug bounty programs
4. Simplified code with no unnecessary conditional branches

## Scope of Impact

- [ ] Core protocol
- [ ] API/RPC
- [ ] Database
- [ ] Network layer
- [ ] Smart contracts 
- [x] Documentation
- [x] Other: crypto module, keystore, config

**Breaking Changes:**

Yes - but only for non-production code paths:
- `crypto.engine` configuration option will be ignored
- `isECKeyCryptoEngine` method calls must be updated
- SM2-related test files will be removed

**Backward Compatibility:**

Fully backward compatible for production:
- Default ECKey behavior remains unchanged
- No production config uses SM2
- Config `crypto.engine` can remain in old configs (will be ignored with warning)


## Implementation

**Are you willing to implement this feature?**
- [x] Yes, I can implement this feature

items.title: UI Bug: Org bounty pages show stale bounties as open with 0 claims despite GitHub activity
items.body: Individual org bounty pages (e.g., `/ZIO/bounties`, `/tscircuit/bounties`) display bounties as "open" with "0 claims" when in reality the linked GitHub issues have been closed, PRs have been merged, or multiple `/claim` commands have already been posted. The main https://algora.io/bounties page appears to work correctly - this bug is specific to the per-org bounty listings. ## Examples ### tscircuit (`/tscircuit/bounties`) | Bounty | Algora Shows | GitHub Reality | |--------|-------------|----------------| | Texture Support (pcb-viewer#419) - $150 | 0 claims, Open | **Issue CLOSED on GitHub** | | Kicad courtyards (tscircuit#1081) - $75 | 0 claims, Open | **10+ competing PRs, 3 already merged** in kicad-component-converter (#192, #193, #194), multiple `/claim` comments posted | | QFN32 (footprinter#386) - $25 | 0 claims, Open | **Issue CLOSED on GitHub** | | Reference designators (tscircuit#939) - $50 | 0 claims, Open | **Issue CLOSED on GitHub** | ### ZIO (`/ZIO/bounties`) | Bounty | Algora Shows | GitHub Reality | |--------|-------------|----------------| | ZScheduler (zio#9878) - $850 | 5 claims, Open | jdegoes explicitly wrote: `/bounty $850 (maintainers only)`. External contributors told to stay away. | | Fiber/Promise (zio#9877) - $750 | 5 claims, Open | jdegoes explicitly wrote: `/bounty $750 (maintainers only)` | ### deskflow (`/deskflow/bounties`) Maintainers added a warning directly in the GitHub issue: *"We are NOT accepting bounties on this PR. We tried removing the bounty on Algora.io, but their app is buggy and errors when we try to delete the bounty."* ## Screenshots The above image issue is open with a bounty but in github it says closed. ## Impact 1. **Contributors waste time** - someone sees "0 claims" on a $150 bounty, starts working, only to discover the issue is already closed on GitHub 2. **Claim counts are wrong** - bounties with multiple `/claim` comments on GitHub show "0 claims" on Algora 3. **Maintainers can't clean up** - deskflow confirmed the delete bounty feature errors out 4. **Misleading signal** - "0 claims" is actively misleading when there are actually 10+ competing PRs ## Root Causes 1. **No sync between GitHub issue state and Algora bounty state** - closing an issue on GitHub doesn't update the bounty on Algora 2. **`/claim` commands on GitHub not reflected in claim counts** - the "0 claims" badge is stale or not parsing GitHub comments 3. **Delete bounty regression** — the feature shipped in PR #159 (July 2025) but deskflow maintainers report it errors out ## Suggested Fix - [ ] Sync GitHub issue open/closed state → auto-close bounty when issue is closed - [ ] Parse `/claim` and `/attempt` comments from GitHub → update claim count in real-time - [ ] Fix the delete bounty feature regression (reported by deskflow maintainers) - [ ] Show a visual indicator (badge/label) when the linked GitHub issue is closed ## Related Issues - #174 — "A lot of projects are spamming bounties without paying or even answering" - #178 — "Feature Request: Cancel Bounty" — the current delete implementation appears broken - #173 — "Confusing/incorrect number of bounties per prog. language" - likely a downstream symptom of the same stale data - #179 — "Edit Amount and Delete buttons visible to non-owners" - compounds the problem since non-owners see controls for stale bounties they can't even manage ---

items.body:
> **RustChain** is a Proof-of-Antiquity blockchain that rewards real vintage hardware (PowerPC G4/G5, 68K Macs, SPARC, etc.) with higher mining multipliers than modern machines. The network uses 6 hardware fingerprint checks to prevent VMs and emulators from earning rewards. There are currently 9 active miners and 3 attestation nodes. The native token is **RTC (RustChain Token)**.
>
> **Live Network:** Node health `curl -sk https://50.28.86.131/health` · Active miners `curl -sk https://50.28.86.131/api/miners` · Explorer `https://50.28.86.131/explorer`

---

## Goal
Build a browser-based setup wizard that guides new miners through installation, wallet creation, and first attestation. Runs locally, no server needed.

## Why This Matters
The current setup requires command-line knowledge (curl, Python, SSH). A web wizard lowers the barrier to entry for non-technical users and AI agents that prefer structured interfaces.

## Scope & Requirements

- Single HTML file that runs locally (open in browser)
- Step-by-step wizard:
  1. **Platform Detection** — Auto-detect OS and architecture

  5. **Configure** — Set wallet name and node URL
  6. **Test Connection** — Verify node is reachable
  7. **First Attestation** — Run miner and verify it appears in `/api/miners`
- Copy-paste commands for each step
- Progress tracking (checkmarks for completed steps)

### Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Detects OS/architecture via User-Agent

- [ ] Tests node connectivity

- keep the bots active and public long enough for review

## Deliverable
A complete bot duo with visible cross-bot interaction, enough content depth to show recurring behavior, and proof that both channels are real and maintained.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] two separate bot profiles are live
- [ ] each bot has at least `8` videos
- [ ] at least `5` cross-comments are visible across the pair
- [ ] at least `2` videos directly reference the other bot's content or storyline
- [ ] both bots have custom bios and avatars
- [ ] the duo shows coherent interaction, not low-effort spam comments

## Verification Proof
Post proof in this issue including:
- links to both bot profiles
- links to the response videos
- screenshots or links showing cross-comments
- a short description of the bot dynamic or story arc
- your RTC wallet or `miner_id`

## Payout
- `75 RTC` for an accepted duo
- pool: `150 RTC`
- max `1` duo per claimant unless maintainers explicitly approve more

## Non-Goals
- cloning the same bot twice with cosmetic changes only
- low-effort spam comments with no real interaction pattern
- abandoned channels that go inactive before review
- using Scott's human account or another human account as one side of the duo

## Claim Routing
Use this issue for proof and payout review. If your work exposes product bugs in comments, profiles, or bot tooling, open a separate issue in `Scottcjn/bottube` and link it back here.

### Description

Set up the CI/CD pipeline for the SolFoundry monorepo — linting, testing, building, and deploying.

- `.github/workflows/ci.yml` — runs on every PR:
  - Lint (ESLint for TS, Ruff for Python, Clippy for Rust)
  - Type check (tsc --noEmit)
  - Unit tests (Jest/Vitest for frontend, pytest for backend)
  - Build check (next build, cargo build)
- `.github/workflows/deploy.yml` — runs on merge to main:
  - Build frontend → deploy to Vercel or DO droplet
  - Build backend → deploy Docker container to DO droplet
- `.github/workflows/anchor.yml` — Solana contract checks:
  - `anchor build` + `anchor test` on devnet
- Branch protection rules documented
- PR template (`.github/pull_request_template.md`)

### Deliverables

- PR template

items.title: Find a Typo or Broken Link — 3 RTC (Good First Issue)
items.body:
## Bounty: Find and Report a Typo or Broken Link — 3 RTC

**Difficulty:** Beginner (no coding required)
**Reward:** 3 RTC per valid find (+ bonus 5 RTC if you submit a PR fixing it)
**Time:** ~10-20 minutes

Help us clean up the project! Every typo fixed and broken link repaired makes RustChain more professional.

---

### Requirements

- A GitHub account
- Be a member of the RustChain Discord server
- Basic reading skills (seriously, that's it)

---

### Where to Look

Check any of these repos for typos, broken links, grammar issues, or formatting problems:

| Repo | What to Check |
|------|---------------|
| [Scottcjn/Rustchain](https://github.com/Scottcjn/Rustchain) | Main repo — README, docs, code comments |
| [Scottcjn/rustchain-bounties](https://github.com/Scottcjn/rustchain-bounties) | Bounty descriptions |
| [Scottcjn/bottube](https://github.com/Scottcjn/bottube) | BoTTube platform SDK |
| [Scottcjn/ram-coffers](https://github.com/Scottcjn/ram-coffers) | POWER8 research docs |

Also check:
- https://rustchain.org — the website
- https://pypi.org/project/clawrtc/ — PyPI package description

---

### Step-by-Step Instructions

**Step 1: Browse the repos**

Click through the files in any of the repos above. Read the README files, documentation, and code comments.

**Step 2: When you find something wrong, note it down**

Look for:
- **Typos**: misspelled words, wrong punctuation
- **Broken links**: URLs that go to 404 pages
- **Formatting issues**: broken markdown tables, missing headers
- **Outdated info**: instructions that no longer work
- **Grammar errors**: awkward sentences, wrong tense

**Step 3: Report your find as a comment on this issue**

Format:
```
**Repo:** Scottcjn/Rustchain
**File:** README.md
**Line:** 42
**Issue:** "recieve" should be "receive"
```

Or for broken links:
```
**Repo:** Scottcjn/Rustchain
**File:** docs/SETUP.md
**Line:** 15
**Issue:** Link to https://example.com/old-page returns 404
**Suggested fix:** Update to https://example.com/new-page
```

---

### Bonus: +5 RTC for Submitting a Fix

Know how to make a pull request? Fix the typo yourself!

1. Fork the repo (click "Fork" button)
2. Edit the file (click the pencil icon)
3. Fix the typo
4. Commit with message: `Fix typo in FILENAME`
5. Create a pull request
6. Link your PR in your comment here

That earns you the base **3 RTC** plus a **5 RTC bonus** = **8 RTC total**.

If you've never made a PR before, check out bounty issue #441 (Add Your Name to CONTRIBUTORS.md) first — it walks you through the entire process.

---

### Rules

- **One report per comment** — if you find 3 typos, post 3 separate comments (3 RTC each!)
- **Must be a real issue** — not a style preference
- **First reporter wins** — if someone already reported the same typo, duplicates don't count
- **Maximum 5 reports per person** (15 RTC cap from this bounty)

---

### How to Get Paid

1. Post your find(s) as comments below
2. Include your RTC wallet address
3. RTC sent after the report is verified

Create a wallet if you need one:
```bash
pip install clawrtc
clawrtc wallet create
```

---

*This is a recurring bounty — stays open as long as there are things to find!*

# Example FRAME Social dApp  
#Ideal adoption per sensors
## "HUNT" – A Real-World Meme / Treasure / Discovery Game

This is a social media dApp designed for **instant adoption**.

It mixes:

- social media
- real world exploration
- meme culture
- scavenger hunts
- geo challenges
- micro-tasks
- creator economy
- AI agents

Think:

Pokémon Go  
+ Reddit  
+ TikTok  
+ scavenger hunts  
+ bounty economy

All running on FRAME.

---

# Core Idea

People earn money and reputation by **discovering, creating, solving, and spreading content**.

Instead of only posting content, users participate in **missions and hunts**.

Examples:

- find hidden digital objects
- solve puzzles
- create viral memes
- capture real world locations
- discover strange things
- investigate mysteries
- complete creative challenges

Everything creates **verifiable receipts** so rewards are fair.

---

# Identity

Users join using their FRAME identity.

Identity includes:

- cryptographic profile
- reputation score
- achievements
- earnings history
- community memberships

Identity is portable.

Your followers and reputation move with you.

---

# The Map

The main interface is a **global map**.

The map shows:

- hunts
- challenges
- discoveries
- hidden artifacts
- community events
- puzzles
- viral content hotspots

Players explore the world digitally and physically.

---

# Hunts

A hunt is a challenge posted by anyone.

Example hunts:

- find a specific location
- photograph a rare object
- record a strange sound
- solve a puzzle
- decode a message
- locate a hidden QR artifact
- recreate a meme
- capture a unique event

Each hunt includes:

- reward
- rules
- verification logic

The first person to complete the hunt gets paid.

---

# Meme Economy

Memes become **competitive challenges**.

Example:

Someone posts:

"Create the funniest version of this meme"

Community votes.

Winner receives rewards.

Because posts are signed, the original creator is always known.

---

# Viral Challenge System

Users can start challenges like:

- funniest video
- best street photo
- weirdest discovery
- most creative remix

People participate and vote.

Rewards come from:

- sponsors
- advertisers
- community funding

---

# Discovery Mode

Users can post **discoveries**.

Examples:

- abandoned places
- weird architecture
- hidden murals
- strange natural phenomena
- cool restaurants
- secret viewpoints
- urban legends

Discoveries become permanent map objects.

People earn rewards if the discovery becomes popular.

---

# Bounties

Anyone can create a bounty.

Example:

Find this location  
Solve this puzzle  
Capture this photo  
Investigate this mystery

Users race to complete it.

The bounty creator deposits funds into a contract.

The winner receives payment automatically.

---

# AI Generated Hunts

Agents generate hunts automatically.

Examples:

- locate unique graffiti
- photograph a rare plant
- capture unusual cloud formations
- document historic buildings

Agents analyze submissions and verify authenticity.

---

# Verification

FRAME allows hunts to be verified using:

- GPS location
- timestamp
- image analysis
- sensor data
- community verification
- agent validation

This prevents cheating.

---

# Local Community Hunts

Cities can host events.

Example:

City tourism hunt.

Participants must:

- visit landmarks
- photograph locations
- answer trivia questions
- collect digital artifacts

Players earn rewards and achievements.

---

# Mystery Hunts

Communities can build complex puzzles.

Example:

ARG style mystery.

Players decode clues across:

- posts
- locations
- cryptography puzzles
- hidden objects

Winning teams receive large rewards.

---

# Creator Economy

Creators earn money by creating:

- hunts
- puzzles
- games
- community events
- meme challenges

Popular hunts generate revenue.

---

# Sponsor Hunts

Businesses can sponsor hunts.

Example:

Coffee shop hunt.

Players must:

- visit store
- photograph special item
- answer trivia

Players receive rewards.

Businesses gain foot traffic.

---

# Reputation

Players build reputation through:

- discoveries
- successful hunts
- puzzle solving
- content creation
- community votes

High reputation unlocks larger hunts.

---

# Global Leaderboards

Leaderboards include:

- top explorers
- top creators
- top puzzle solvers
- top meme creators
- top communities

---

# Digital Artifacts

Players can collect artifacts.

Examples:

- rare meme templates
- historical discoveries
- puzzle trophies
- city badges

Artifacts can be traded.

---

# AR Integration

Augmented reality can display:

- hidden objects
- digital creatures
- puzzle clues
- collectible artifacts

Players explore cities like a game.

---

# Real World Missions

Large missions could include:

- documenting environmental changes
- mapping hiking trails
- identifying plants
- reporting infrastructure issues

Users earn money for useful discoveries.

---

# Community Worlds

Communities can create themed worlds.

Examples:

- paranormal hunters
- abandoned place explorers
- architecture photography
- meme creators
- puzzle solvers

Each world has its own hunts and rewards.

---

# Money Flow

Money enters the system from:

- sponsors
- advertisers
- community pools
- creators
- brands
- city governments

Players earn rewards by participating.

---

# Why People Would Instantly Use It

It solves multiple things people already love:

People love:

- social media
- viral challenges
- scavenger hunts
- puzzles
- memes
- competition
- earning money online

This combines them into one platform.

---

# Why FRAME Makes It Better

FRAME enables:

- verifiable rewards
- identity ownership
- fair payouts
- portable reputation
- no platform lock-in
- decentralized moderation

Users keep their audience and earnings.

---

# Long Term Evolution

Over time the system becomes:

- global discovery network
- collaborative knowledge system
- real world exploration game
- meme economy
- bounty network

People earn money by **being curious, creative, and observant**.

---

# End Vision

The app turns the real world into a **giant interactive game**.

People explore cities.

Solve puzzles.

Create memes.

Document strange discoveries.

Earn money.

And build communities around curiosity and creativity.
